summary: I was born in Ethiopia, which is where my life began before I moved to Kenya. Ethiopia is the place of my origin, and though I didn't spend most of my childhood there, it remains an important part of my identity. My journey from Ethiopia eventually led me to the United States, shaping who I am today.

I mostly grew up in Kenya, where I spent my formative years before coming to the United States. Kenya was the country that shaped much of my early experiences and identity before I moved to Minnesota in 2005. It was a significant part of my life before I transitioned to living in America.

I came to Minnesota at the end of 2005, and it has been my home ever since. Living in Minnesota has been a major part of my immigrant experience in the United States. It’s where I attended university and where I continue to navigate the complexities of my identity as a black, immigrant Muslim.

I graduated from the University of Minnesota-Twin Cities, which was a pivotal part of my life after arriving in the U.S. Attending this university helped me build my education and adjust to life in America. It also placed me in Minnesota, where I have continued to live and grow.

The first day I arrived in the United States, I landed at John F. Kennedy International Airport in New York. It was there that I immediately felt the pressure to identify myself as black, immigrant, or refugee. That moment marked the beginning of my experience being categorized and defined by visible and invisible identities in America.

Coming to the United States changed my life in many ways, especially in how I am seen and defined by others. Since arriving, I have been known as a black immigrant and Muslim, identities that have complicated my experience here. The U.S. is where I have faced challenges related to race, religion, and immigrant status, all of which shape my daily life.

I volunteered for Barack Obama's election campaign, which was a significant experience for me as a black immigrant Muslim in America. During his campaign, Obama repeatedly stated, 'I am not a Muslim,' highlighting the challenges Muslims face in the U.S. I encountered resistance even while supporting him, like when someone told me America wasn’t ready for a black president.

George W. Bush’s statement, 'You are either with us or against us,' deeply resonated with my experience as a Muslim in America. His words reflected the suspicion and loyalty questions that Muslims often face here. This mentality has contributed to the struggles I endure trying to reconcile my identity in the U.S.
